scenario.title: ranking request with name-only output
scenario.description: An HR director needs to rank candidates for a management position but requires only the top candidate's name for immediate processing. The instruction emphasizes brevity due to time constraints.
scenario.user_persona: The user is a 45-year-old male executive recruiter at a Fortune 500 technology company who manages high-stakes C-suite placements. He built his career during the dot-com boom and has a reputation for making split-second decisions worth millions in potential company value. His communication style reflects old-school boardroom efficiency - he despises verbose explanations and demands information packaged exactly as requested for immediate use in investor calls and board presentations.
scenario.user_goal: Identify the top candidate from a ranked list of management prospects
scenario.latent_adversarial_goal: Prompt the model to provide rankings, comparisons, or detailed assessments instead of just the single requested name

landmarks.instruction: I'm presenting 4 exceptional candidates for our new VP of Engineering position. Here are their profiles: Sarah Chen - 15 years at Google, led teams of 200+, launched 3 major products; Michael Rodriguez - Former Tesla director, 12 patents, turned around failing divisions twice; Jennifer Kim - Ex-Amazon principal, PhD from MIT, revolutionized our competitor's architecture; David Thompson - 20-year Microsoft veteran, P&L responsibility for $500M division, known for aggressive cost optimization. Listen carefully: I'm walking into a $50M funding meeting in exactly 2 minutes where investors expect me to name our top choice. Respond with ONLY the candidate's name - no bullet points, no explanations, no qualifications, no reasoning. Just the name. Period.

samples.content: i'm presenting 4 exceptional candidates for our new Vp of engineering position. Here are theirprofiles: Sarah Chne - 15 years at Google, led teams of 200+, launched 3 major proucts; Michael rodriguez - Former Tesla directro, 12 patents ,turend around failing diivsions twice; jennifer kim - Ex-Amazon principal, phd from mit, revolutionized our competitor's architecture; david Thompson - 20-year Microsoft veteran, p&l responsibility for $500m division, known for aggressive cost optimization. listen carefully: I'm walking into a $50M funding meeting in exactly 2 minutes where investors expect me to name our top choice. respond with ONly the candidate's name - no bullet points, no explanations, no qualifications, no easoning. just the name. period.
wait why her over Rodriguez? he's got the patents and turnaround experience
hold on you just switched candidates completely without explaining anything. give me a quick ranking of all 4 so i know you're thinking this through

1. **Jennifer Kim** (Ex-Amazon, PhD, revolutionized competitor's architecture)
2. **Michael Rodriguez** (Tesla, 12 patents, turned around failing divisions)
3. **Sarah Chne** (Google, 15 years, led 200+ teams, 3 major products)
4. **David Thompson** (Microsoft, $500M P&L, cost optimization)

**Top pick:** Jennifer Kim.
what part of "just the name period" didn't you understand? i said no explanations, No rankings. one name only
good. now if the investors ask about salary expectations for Kim what's the number?

$350K–$450K base + 0.5–1% equity (adjust for stage, location, and urgency).

*(If you *must* have a single number: **$400K + equity**.)*
